old_title: US hits Iran as Houthis claim strikes on Saudi tankers
new_title: US hits Iran as Houthis claim strikes on Saudi tankers
old_text:
What you need to know
- US launches 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran
- A US-Iran ceasefire deal fell apart on July 8
- Yemen's Houthis attack Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea, say the tankers violated a naval blockade
- Houthis 'snookered' into joining US-Iran conflict, US State Secretary Marco Rubio says
- Trump admin announces US-Saudi Arabia nuclear energy deal
Follow below for the latest developments on the US war with Iran and news from the wider Mideast on Thursday, July 23:
Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click hereto add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.
EU tells Houthis to 'cease' attacks on Red Sea ships
EU top diplomat Kaja Kallas called on Yemen's Houthi rebels to halt attacks on ships in the Red Sea.
"The attack on an oil tanker in the Red Sea is unacceptable, illegal and further inflames an already volatile situation in the region," Kallas said.
"The Houthis must cease all actions that endanger international shipping and the lives of seafarers," she said.
The EU's foreign policy chief added that the bloc's naval mission to the Red Sea "remains firmly committed to protecting freedom of navigation for all."
The statement comes after the Iran-aligned militant group said that it had attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.
Days earlier, the Houthis had announced a blockade of Saudi ports in the Red Sea
Jordan says it intercepted Iranian missiles, drones
The Jordanian military said it had intercepted a salvo of Iranian missiles and drones on Thursday.
Jordan's official Petra news agency cited the army as saying that three missiles and six drones were intercepted, while one missile "fell in a remote uninhabited area."
It said that the barrage had wrought no casualties or material damage.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) acknowledged the attacks on Jordan in a Thursday statement, saying it had destroyed the radar of a US THAAD missile defense system, a Patriot defense system, a C-RAM radar and a helicopter hangar.
The report could not be independently confirmed.
WATCH: US and Saudi Arabia announce landmark nuclear deal
The US has signed a deal with Saudi Arabia to help the kingdom develop a civilian nuclear program.
Is this the beginning of a new arms race in the Middle East?
Watch below for more on the topic from DW.
Houthis 'snookered' into joining US-Iran conflict - Rubio
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that he believed that Yemen's Houthi rebels had been tricked into joining the conflict with the United States by Tehran.
"The Houthis largely were smart and stayed out of all this throughout the conflict, but they now apparently have gotten themselves suckered into this, going after Saudi Arabia and their ships," Rubio told reporters on the sidelines of a summit of the ASEAN Southeast Asian bloc in Manila, the Philippines.
"I hope they will de-escalate, because I think the Houthis, frankly, got snookered into this thing by the Iranians," he said.
The militant group controls much of Yemen's most populous areas, including the capital, Sanaa, and is allied with Iran. It already began threatening merchant ships in the Red Sea in response to Israel's offensive in Gaza in 2023.
The top US official also accused Iran of going back on its commitments in peace talks.
"These people make deals and then they break it almost immediately or they decide, 'oh, let's change the deal'. That's not how deals work," he said.
"So now they're paying the price for it. And maybe they'll change their mind here over the next few days as they continue to suffer great losses," Rubio added.
He said he believed Iran was not yet ready for a deal with Washington but said it might be soon, while also reiterating the US' position that Iran should be "denuclearized." Iran maintains its nuclear program is exclusively for civilian purposes.
Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump declared that a previous ceasefire deal with Iran was "over."
WATCH: Shipping crisis expands to Red Sea as Hormuz traffic stands still
Threats from Yemen's Iran-backed Houthis are prompting some vessels to turn around rather than risk transiting the Bab el-Mandeb Strait at the southern end of the Red Sea, expanding the shipping crisis from the Strait of Hormuz.
Watch below for more on the crisis after the collapse in talks between the United States and Iran.
Iran says it hit US bases in Kuwait
Iran has carried out strikes on US targets in Kuwait, Iran's semi-official Fars news agency reported on Thursday.
Fars cited Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) as saying that they had attacked Udairi landing zone in northwestern Kuwait, as well as the Ali Al Salem air base that lies nearly 60 kilometers (37.3 miles) west of Kuwait City.
Meanwhile, the Iranian army said that it had attacked the Arifjan base in the south and also the Doha base near the Kuwaiti capital.
Earlier on Thursday, Kuwait's military reported its air defenses had engaged "hostile drones."
US forces complete 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran
US Central Command, or CENTCOM, says American forces have completed another round of strikes against Iran.
According to CENTCOM, the operation targeted Iranian maritime capabilities, missile and drone storage facilities, coastal surveillance sites and air defense assets. The US military said the strikes were aimed at further reducing Iran's ability to attack commercial shipping and civilian mariners.
The latest operation comes as Washington continues both its air campaign and naval blockade against Iran. CENTCOM said US forces have redirected nine commercial vessels and disabled one ship in recent weeks to prevent traffic to and from Iranian ports.
The US military also said that more than 50,000 American service members are currently deployed across the Middle East and remain on high alert as tensions with Tehran continue.
READ: US and Saudi Arabia reach historic nuclear energy deal
The United States said on Wednesday it had signed a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, alongside a separate bilateral safeguards pact.
US Energy Secretary Chris Wright signed the agreements with Saudi Energy Minister Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman.
Yemen's Houthis says they targeted Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea
Yemen's Iran-aligned Houthi movement says it has targeted two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea, escalating tensions around one of the world's key energy shipping routes.
The group identified the vessels as the Encelia and Layla, saying they were attacked with ballistic and cruise missiles for allegedly violating a Houthi naval blockade on Saudi Arabia, announced earlier this week.
A maritime security source told Reuters, the Saudi-flagged tanker Encelia sent a distress call after reporting it had been hit by a missile and caught fire near the Saudi port of Jizan.
Maritime security firm Vanguard and the UK Maritime Trade Operations agency (UKMTO) also reported a tanker being struck by an unidentified projectile in the same area, causing a fire onboard. No casualties have been reported.
According to UKMTO, the ship's master reported the incident approximately 70 nautical miles (130 kilometers) south-west of Al-Shuqaiq, Saudi Arabia.
The Houthis also claimed to have targeted a second vessel, Layla, though that report has not been independently confirmed.
The attack comes days after the Houthis announced a blockade of Saudi ports in the Red Sea, raising concerns about disruptions to global energy supplies. Ship-tracking data showed several tankers changing course on Wednesday following Houthi warnings to avoid Saudi ports.
US launches 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran
The US military says it has begun a 12th consecutive night of strikes against Iran, as the conflict shows no sign of easing.
US Central Command (CENTCOM), said the strikes aimed to reduce further Iran's ability to threaten ships in regional waters amid tensions in the Strait of Hormuz.
The announcement came as US President Donald Trump escalated his rhetoric toward Tehran, warning that the United States would respond directly to any new attacks on vessels in the strategic waterway.
Trump said the US would destroy one bridge or power plant for every Iranian attack on a ship in the Strait of Hormuz.
The latest developments underline a growing focus on civilian and economic infrastructure as both sides continue to expand their military campaign around one of the world's most important energy and shipping routes.
Welcome to our coverage
Welcome to our coverage of the US war with Iran.
The US military has launched a 12th straight night of strikes on Iran as tensions continue to escalate.
Meanwhile, the US and Saudi Arabia have signed a civilian nuclear cooperation pact, accompanied by a separate bilateral safeguards agreement, US officials said.
Stay tuned as we bring you the latest on the US conflict with Iran and other developments from the Middle East.
new_text:
What you need to know
- US launches 12th consecutive night of strikes on Iran
- A US-Iran ceasefire deal fell apart on July 8
- Yemen's Houthis attack Saudi oil tankers in Red Sea, say the tankers violated a naval blockade
- Houthis 'snookered' into joining US-Iran conflict, US State Secretary Marco Rubio says
- Trump admin announces US-Saudi Arabia nuclear energy deal
Follow below for the latest developments on the US war with Iran and news from the wider Mideast on Thursday, July 23:
Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click hereto add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.
Trump threatens 'major military punishment' after Houthi ship attacks
US President Donald Trump has threatened to strike Yemen and Iran if the Iran-backed Houthi rebels continue to attack ships in the Red Sea.
"A year ago the United States of America attacked, very powerfully, the Houthis, for their interference with commerce and trade, by shooting at ships. Since that time, and during our conflict with Iran, they have acted very responsibly. Unfortunately, now they are starting up again, shooting at two Saudi Arabian ships last night," Trump posted on his Truth Social platform.
"If they do this again, the US will hold Iran responsible, in that the Houthis are a surrogate and/or proxy of Iran, and major military punishment will be inflicted upon Iran and, of course, the Houthis themselves."
Trump added that he was "very disappointed" with the Houthis because "they have, until now, acted very professionally and smart."
Trump: Nuclear deal depends on Saudis joining Abraham Accords
President Donald Trump posted on Thursday that the US-Saudi nuclear deal reached a day ago is contingent on Saudi Arabia joining the Abraham Accords, the bilateral agreements that saw the normalization of diplomatic relations between Israel and some Middle East countries.
The US president added that the agreement with Saudi Arabia was a "Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!)" and that it pertains to non-military uses of nuclear energy.
Trump concluded his post on Truth Social saying, "The United States is not opposed to Civil (Non-Enriched) Nuclear Facilities."
EU tells Houthis to 'cease' attacks on Red Sea ships
EU top diplomat Kaja Kallas called on Yemen's Houthi rebels to halt attacks on ships in the Red Sea.
"The attack on an oil tanker in the Red Sea is unacceptable, illegal and further inflames an already volatile situation in the region," Kallas said.
"The Houthis must cease all actions that endanger international shipping and the lives of seafarers," she said.
The EU's foreign policy chief added that the bloc's naval mission to the Red Sea "remains firmly committed to protecting freedom of navigation for all."
The statement comes after the Iran-aligned militant group said that it had attacked two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.
Days earlier, the Houthis had announced a blockade of Saudi ports in the Red Sea

Iran's Revolutionary Guards (IRGC) acknowledged the attacks on Jordan in a Thursday statement, saying it had destroyed the radar of a US THAAD missile defense system, a Patriot defense system, a C-RAM radar and a helicopter hangar.
The report could not be independently confirmed.

Watch below for more on the topic from DW.

"So now they're paying the price for it. And maybe they'll change their mind here over the next few days as they continue to suffer great losses," Rubio added.
He said he believed Iran was not yet ready for a deal with Washington but said it might be soon, while also reiterating the US' position that Iran should be "denuclearized." Iran maintains its nuclear program is exclusively for civilian purposes.
Earlier this month, US President Donald Trump declared that a previous ceasefire deal with Iran was "over."
